confirmed.title: Quitting MAD from the camera-tuning page orphans the ffmpeg preview and leaves the Sinden lightgun dead
confirmed.description: App.quit() (wired as GamepadNav on_quit, line 865) only calls self._gp_cleanup() and self._cam_restore_driver(), then self.root.destroy(). It does NOT call self._clear(), and crucially does NOT call self._cam_kill_ffmpeg(). The camera-preview ffmpeg is spawned with start_new_session=True (line 2455-2460), so it does NOT die when the parent exits. The global Start+Select hold-to-quit fires from ANY page (handled in GamepadNav._handle, line 626-632), so a user who quits MAD while a camera preview is live (Lightgun -> Camera tuning -> Preview P1/P2, line 2046/2380/2438) orphans the ffmpeg process. Because the UVC camera is a single-opener (see comment at line 2330) and quit() then calls _cam_restore_driver() -> _sinden_restart() while ffmpeg still holds the camera, the Sinden driver restart cannot acquire the camera. By contrast, the normal page-leave path _clear() does it correctly: _cam_kill_ffmpeg() FIRST (line 1375) then _cam_restore_driver() (line 1376).
confirmed.impact: After quitting MAD from the camera-tuning page, an orphaned ffmpeg keeps the V4L2 camera open and the Sinden lightgun stays non-functional (aiming dead) until the user finds and kills the stray ffmpeg by hand. There is also no try/finally around root.mainloop() in main() (line 4601-4606) to catch this.
confirmed.fix: In quit() call self._cam_kill_ffmpeg() BEFORE self._cam_restore_driver() (mirroring _clear's ordering), or simply call self._clear() at the top of quit() so every page's teardown (camera ffmpeg, Daphne capture subprocess, tester grabs) runs deterministically before root.destroy().

verify.reasoning: quit() at router-config-gui.py:1870-1875 calls self._gp_cleanup() then self._cam_restore_driver() then self.root.destroy() — it does NOT call self._cam_kill_ffmpeg(). _cam_restore_driver (1930-1942) calls _sinden_restart (1937) when the driver was running. The preview ffmpeg is spawned with start_new_session=True (2455-2460) so it survives the parent and keeps the single-opener UVC camera (comment at 2330 'UVC single opener') busy, so the Sinden restart can't reacquire it. The correct teardown in _clear() does kill-then-restore in the right order (1375 _cam_kill_ffmpeg, then 1376 _cam_restore_driver). The hold-to-quit timer fires on_quit from ANY page (GamepadNav._handle line 632), and the camera page Preview buttons (2380 -> _cam_preview 2438) are reachable. The code comment at 2331 even claims quit handles this ('restarts it on Save / on any page-exit (see _clear + quit)'), but quit does not kill ffmpeg first. Confirmed real bug.

confirmed.title: model-2-emulator.sh sed targets literal "M2CONFIGFILE" instead of a variable — DrawCross crosshair toggle is dead
confirmed.description: Lines 27 and 30 run `sed -i 's/DrawCross=1/DrawCross=0/' "M2CONFIGFILE"` and `sed -i 's/DrawCross=0/DrawCross=1/' "M2CONFIGFILE"`. `M2CONFIGFILE` is a bare literal string, not a variable reference (`$M2CONFIGFILE`/`${M2CONFIGFILE}`), and no such variable is defined anywhere in the script (verified with grep — only these two lines mention it). sed therefore tries to edit a file literally named `M2CONFIGFILE` in the current working directory (which is `$romsPath/model2` after the `cd` at line 59 — but the sed runs BEFORE that cd, so cwd is ES-DE's launch dir). The file doesn't exist, sed fails with 'No such file or directory', and because the script has no `set -e` the failure is silently ignored. The sibling script model2-m2emu.sh does the identical logic correctly with `"$INI"` (line 28-29).
confirmed.impact: The Model 2 Emulator crosshair is never hidden for the gun games (bel/gunblade/rchase2) nor re-enabled for other games — DrawCross stays at whatever the .ini already had. Lightgun games show a doubled/wrong crosshair; the per-game toggle is completely non-functional.
confirmed.fix: Define the variable (e.g. `M2CONFIGFILE="$romsPath/model2/EMULATOR.INI"`) and reference it as `"$M2CONFIGFILE"`, and move the sed AFTER the path is known. Mirror model2-m2emu.sh which already does this correctly.

verify.reasoning: model-2-emulator.sh:27 and :30 run sed against the bare literal string "M2CONFIGFILE" (no $ / ${}), and grep confirms no M2CONFIGFILE variable is defined anywhere — only these two lines mention it. There is NO `set -e`/`set -u` in the script (verified), so sed's 'No such file' failure is silently ignored. The cd to $romsPath/model2 is at line 59, AFTER the sed at 27/30, so cwd at sed time is ES-DE's launch dir, where no file named M2CONFIGFILE exists. The sibling model2-m2emu.sh:28-29 does the identical toggle correctly with "$INI" (defined line 17) and `set -eu` (line 14). The DrawCross crosshair toggle is therefore completely dead — a real functional bug on the lightgun-game path. High severity is justified.

confirmed.title: mad-backend teardown never shuts down the slow-method ThreadPool, so a SIGTERM/EOF can hang the daemon (and orphan a hypseus_capture SDL subprocess)
confirmed.description: _POOL = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) runs every slow=True method (backup.snapshot, sinden.status pgrep, preview SDL init, daphne.bind, etc.). On panel death the chain is: SIGTERM/EOF -> _on_term raises KeyboardInterrupt -> main loop breaks -> finally calls stop_all_streams(). stop_all_streams() ONLY stops Stream objects (rpc.py:147-156); it never calls _POOL.shutdown(). ThreadPoolExecutor worker threads are NON-daemon, and the executor registers an atexit handler that JOINS them on interpreter shutdown. So if any slow method is mid-flight when the panel dies, main() returns but sys.exit() blocks in concurrent.futures' atexit until that task completes — e.g. daphne.bind does subprocess.Popen(hypseus_capture.py) + communicate(timeout=14) (daphne_cmds.py:191-193), so teardown stalls up to ~14s. Worse, daphne.bind's hypseus_capture.py child is NOT a Stream and is never killed by stop_all_streams, so during that window the SDL-holding capture subprocess is orphaned and the 'input.lock locked:true' it emitted is the last thing the (now-dead) panel heard.
confirmed.impact: On a normal ES-DE/panel exit while a backup, SDL probe, or daphne button-capture is running, the backend hangs for seconds instead of exiting promptly, and a hypseus_capture SDL subprocess can be left running after the daemon is supposed to be gone — undermining the documented 'no child/grab survives teardown' invariant.
confirmed.fix: In the finally block of mad-backend.main(), after stop_all_streams(), call rpc-level teardown that does _POOL.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True) and track/kill any subprocess spawned by slow methods (daphne.bind should register its Popen so teardown can kill it, or run it as a Stream like the camera/backup streams). At minimum make the pool threads daemon=True so sys.exit can't block on them.

verify.reasoning: rpc.py:24 _POOL = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) runs every slow=True method (rpc.py:82-83). mad-backend.py:159-162 finally only calls stop_all_streams() (rpc.py:147-156, which only stops Stream objects); it never calls _POOL.shutdown(). Verified in CPython 3.13: concurrent.futures.thread registers _python_exit via threading._register_atexit and joins worker threads (t.join()), and the comment confirms workers are non-daemon. daphne.bind is slow=True (daphne_cmds.py:174) and does subprocess.Popen(hypseus_capture.py)+communicate(timeout=14) (daphne_cmds.py:191-193). So if a slow method is mid-flight at panel death, sys.exit() (mad-backend.py:167) blocks in the atexit join up to ~14s, and the hypseus_capture child is a plain Popen, not a Stream, so stop_all_streams never kills it. Resource-leak/hang on a normal exit path; high is correct.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py rewrites steam/gamelist.xml with NO ES-DE-running guard (its sibling has one)
confirmed.description: main() reads GL = ~/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml (line 215), writes a timestamped .bak (line 217), then GL.write_text(new) (line 252) with the metadata-rewritten content. Project rule: never edit gamelist.xml while ES-DE is up, because ES-DE rewrites gamelists on exit and clobbers external changes. If this script runs while ES-DE is open (entirely possible — the user can shell in or trigger it from a tool), the metadata write is silently lost on ES-DE exit, OR ES-DE's in-memory copy overwrites it.
confirmed.impact: Running the Steam metadata fetch while ES-DE is open either loses all the scraped metadata/cover work on ES-DE exit or risks an inconsistent gamelist; the timestamped .bak only protects the PRE-edit state, not the lost edits.
confirmed.fix: Add the same pgrep -x es-de/emulationstation guard steam-collection-sync.py uses and refuse to write (or warn loudly) when ES-DE is running; the write is already backed up but the guard is the real protection.

verify.reasoning: steam-fetch-metadata.py reads GL=~/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml (line 37/215), writes a timestamped .bak (217), and rewrites the live gamelist with GL.write_text(new) (252). grep across the whole file finds NO pgrep/ES-DE-running guard — only doc-comment mentions of ES-DE. confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py crashes on any non-Steam launcher .sh (unguarded regex .group)
confirmed.description: Line 207 does `rg = int(re.search(r"rungameid/(\d+)", Path(sh).read_text()).group(1))` with NO None-check. If any `.sh` in ~/ROMs/steam lacks a `rungameid/<digits>` string, re.search returns None and `.group(1)` raises AttributeError, aborting the entire metadata run before it writes anything. Both sibling scripts handle this correctly: steam-collection-sync.py:91-93 and steam-fetch-media.py:206-208 both do `m = re.search(...); if not m: continue`. steam-collection-sync.py also documents that non-Steam shortcuts (rgid >= 2**32) coexist in the same dir, and a non-Steam shortcut .sh may not contain a rungameid pattern, so this crash is reachable in normal use.

confirmed.fix: Mirror the siblings: `m = re.search(r"rungameid/(\d+)", Path(sh).read_text()); if not m: continue; rg = int(m.group(1))`.

confirmed.title: quit-combo-watcher self-SIGTERMs and breaks the policy's fast KILL escalation (Eden/multi-app quit_cmd)
confirmed.description: _quit() runs the policy quit_cmd verbatim via subprocess.run(quit_cmd, shell=True). For the Eden backend the configured quit_cmd is `pkill -TERM -f 'Eden|Yuzu|Suyu|Ryujinx'; sleep 2; pkill -KILL -f 'Eden|Yuzu|Suyu|Ryujinx'` (controller-policy.toml:415). pkill -f matches the FULL cmdline. The watcher's own process cmdline contains that exact pattern string (it was passed as --quit-cmd), and so does the bash -c child that subprocess.run spawned to run the chain. So the very first `pkill -TERM` sends SIGTERM to (a) the watcher itself and (b) the shell that is supposed to `sleep 2; pkill -KILL` — killing the sleep before the escalation runs. Confirmed live: two distinct watcher/shell processes carry 'Eden' in argv. The detached setsid `trap '' TERM` backstop (lines 113-116) is the ONLY thing that still force-kills Eden, but it fires at KILL_AFTER=6s, not the 2s the policy comment intends.
confirmed.impact: Eden (and any multi-token quit_cmd whose pattern matches the watcher's own argv) takes the global 6s backstop to die instead of the intended 2s grace; the policy's documented 'fast escalate after 2s' path is silently defeated. User sees a ~6s hang on quit-combo before ES-DE returns.
confirmed.fix: Run the quit_cmd in its own detached session so the watcher isn't in the pkill blast radius (e.g. subprocess.run(["setsid","bash","-c",quit_cmd]) and have the watcher exit immediately), or make the chain immune the way the backstop is (`setsid; trap '' TERM` around the whole quit_cmd). Alternatively pass the emulator pattern without it ending up in the watcher's own visible argv.

verify.reasoning: Reproduced the mechanism empirically. The watcher is launched by /home/deck/ES-DE/scripts/game-start/quit-combo-watcher.sh:22-23 with `--quit-cmd "$QUIT"`, and for Eden $QUIT = the chain at controller-policy.toml:415 `pkill -TERM -f 'Eden|Yuzu|Suyu|Ryujinx'; sleep 2; pkill -KILL -f ...`. quit-combo-watcher.py:120 runs it via `subprocess.run(quit_cmd, shell=True)` -> /bin/sh -c (which is bash on SteamOS) whose own argv contains the pattern. I confirmed `pkill -f`/`pgrep -f` matches a `bash -c` shell carrying the pattern in its argv, and a fully-detached repro mirroring subprocess.run(shell=True) showed the chain's `sleep 2; pkill -KILL` escalation NEVER ran (the chain shell SIGTERM'd itself via the first `pkill -TERM`). The watcher's own death is harmless (it returns at line 192-193 anyway), but the inline 2s escalation is defeated; only the detached `setsid; trap '' TERM` backstop (lines 113-116) kills Eden, at KILL_AFTER=6s. So the policy's documented '2s fast escalate' (comment controller-policy.toml:411-414) is silently replaced by a ~6s wait. Medium is fair: no data loss, ~4s extra hang on quit-combo for Eden and any multi-token quit_cmd whose pattern is in the watcher's visible argv.

confirmed.title: Base controller-policy.toml parse is unguarded; a hand-edit typo aborts EVERY game launch
confirmed.description: load_policy() wraps the LOCAL policy parse in try/except (lines 51-56, 'a broken local file must never break routing') but parses the human-edited base POLICY_FILE with no guard (lines 48-50). controller-router.py main() (lines 566-582) calls _setup/_standalone without any top-level try/except, and controller-router-wrap.sh runs `set -euo pipefail` then `"$ROUTER" setup ...` and only execs the emulator if the router exits 0. So a single TOML syntax error in controller-policy.toml raises tomllib.TOMLDecodeError -> router exits non-zero -> wrapper aborts -> ES-DE reports a launch failure. Because setup runs on every launch, the typo blocks ALL games, not just the mis-edited system.
confirmed.impact: One stray character in the base policy makes nothing launchable until the file is fixed, with the only signal being an ES-DE 'launch failed'. Inconsistent with the deliberately fail-soft handling everywhere else (local-file guard, track-view swallow, fallback tokens).
confirmed.fix: Guard the base parse too (catch TOMLDecodeError/OSError, log, fall back to {'systems': {}} so routing degrades to RetroArch defaults), and/or wrap _setup/_standalone in main() so an unexpected router exception logs and returns 0 (let the game launch un-routed) rather than aborting the launch. Keep exit 1 reserved for the intentional Cancel-dialog path.

verify.reasoning: lib/routing.py:48-50 parses the base POLICY_FILE with `with POLICY_FILE.open('rb') as f: base = tomllib.load(f)` and NO try/except, while the LOCAL file IS guarded at 51-56 ('a broken local file must never break routing'). controller-router.py main() (566-582) calls _setup (line 158 -> load_policy()) / _standalone with no top-level try/except, and __main__ does `sys.exit(main(sys.argv))` (586). controller-router-wrap.sh:14,31,34 runs `set -euo pipefail`, `"$ROUTER" setup ...`, then `exec "$@"` only on success. So a single TOML syntax error in the base policy raises TOMLDecodeError -> non-zero exit -> wrapper aborts -> ES-DE launch failure, for EVERY wrapped system (the primary configured path). Note: the unwrapped/bundled-RA hook 04-controller-router-setup.sh:21-22 swallows the failure (`|| echo WARN; exit 0`), so 'blocks ALL games' is precise for wrapped systems but not bundled-only ones. Real fail-soft inconsistency; medium is correct (trigger is a hand-edit typo, consequence is total launch blockage for wrapped systems).

confirmed.title: enumerate_devices() mutates the module-global _ENUM_CACHE with no lock; unsafe under mad-backend's concurrent threads
confirmed.description: _ENUM_CACHE is a process-global dict written at lines 171 (.pop), 193 (cache populate) and 216-217 (prune loop). enumerate_devices() is the single hot path and is called from multiple concurrent contexts in mad-backend: @method(slow=True) handlers on the 4-thread pool (gamepads.list devices.py-callers in device_cmds.py:64/76, preview_cmds.py:142/156) AND background Stream threads (XArcadeTesterStream.run -> dv.enumerate_devices() at tester_cmds.py:748), plus the GUI's 2s GamepadNav poll. There is no _ENUM_LOCK and callers don't serialize. Two concurrent walks interleave: thread A's just-populated _ENUM_CACHE[path] can be deleted by thread B's prune loop (B's `alive` set was built before A added the node), and the read-probe-write sequence per node is not atomic as a unit. The prune list-comprehension snapshot avoids a hard RuntimeError, but the cache thrashes — re-opening evdev nodes the comment explicitly added the cache to AVOID (the 37ms-close / 'MAD lags everywhere' regression).
confirmed.impact: Cache effectiveness collapses under controller hotplug bursts while MAD is open (the exact scenario the cache was built for), re-introducing the multi-hundred-ms per-walk cost and GUI lag; in the worst interleave a node is briefly mis-cached. Router (single-shot process) is unaffected; this only bites the daemon.
confirmed.fix: Add a module-level threading.Lock and hold it around the whole enumerate_devices() body (or at least the cache read/populate + prune), matching the existing _SDL_LOCK pattern in the same file.

verify.reasoning: lib/devices.py enumerate_devices() mutates module-global _ENUM_CACHE at lines 171 (.pop), 193 (populate) and 216-217 (prune del-loop) with no lock — confirmed there is NO _ENUM_LOCK; only _SDL_LOCK (line 394) and a _WII_LOCK (device_cmds.py:105) exist, neither covering the cache. The backend is genuinely multi-threaded: rpc.py:24 ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) for slow methods, fast methods inline on the stdin thread (rpc.py:84-85), plus per-stream threads (rpc.py:107). Concurrent callers confirmed: devices.scan is FAST (device_cmds.py:68 has no slow=True) so runs on stdin thread, while devices.sdl/preview (device_cmds.py:76, preview_cmds.py:142/156) run on the pool and DeviceWatcherStream (device_cmds.py:141) + XArcadeTesterStream (tester_cmds.py:748) run on their own threads — all unserialized. The line-216 list-comprehension snapshot avoids a hard 'dict changed size' RuntimeError, but a thread-A populate at 193 can be deleted by thread-B's stale-`alive` prune at 217, so the cache thrashes and re-probes (the ~37ms-close cost the cache at lines 126-134 exists to avoid). Daemon-only, no crash; medium is right.

confirmed.title: load_merged()'s hand-rolled 2-level merge diverges from the router's recursive deep_merge at 3-level tables (templates / per-system pins)
confirmed.description: load_merged() merges only two dict levels: for a top-level table k it iterates v.items() and does base[k][kk].update(vv) for dict children (line 28) — i.e. it REPLACES the level-3 sub-dict instead of recursing. The router reads the same local.toml via lib/routing.deep_merge (routing.py:35-43), which IS fully recursive. They disagree exactly at the 3-level tables this policy uses: [backends.cemu.templates] (base has two entries 057e:0330 + 054c:0ce6) and [systems.<sys>.pins] (written by policy_cmds.set_pins). Reproduced: base templates={057e:0330,054c:0ce6}, local override {057e:0330: new} -> load_merged yields ONLY {057e:0330:new} (drops 054c:0ce6) while deep_merge yields BOTH. Normal GUI use is masked because set_backend_template/set_pins re-dump the full sub-dict, but a hand-edited or older/partial local.toml carrying a single templates/pins key makes the panel display + backup_targets (load_merged) show a different effective policy than the router actually applies at launch. The module docstring and policy_cmds.py both claim the returned merged view is 'truth' the UI re-renders from; for partial 3-level overrides it is not.
confirmed.impact: GUI/backup show a cemu template map (or per-system pin map) that differs from what the router writes into Cemu/RetroArch at game start; user edits a partial override and the panel misreports the result. Silent, not a crash.
confirmed.fix: Replace the bespoke loop with the same recursive merge the router uses (import routing.deep_merge or inline an identical recursive helper) so load_merged() and load_policy() compute identical results for any nesting depth.

verify.reasoning: Reproduced the divergence with the two real code paths side by side. lib/policy.py load_merged() (24-32) does only a 2-level merge: for child kk it does `base[k][kk].update(vv)` (line 28) — a SHALLOW dict.update that REPLACES the level-3 sub-dict. lib/routing.py deep_merge (35-43) recurses fully. With base backends.cemu.templates={057e:0330,054c:0ce6} (controller-policy.toml:355) and a partial override {057e:0330:'NEW'}, load_merged yields ONLY {057e:0330:'NEW'} (drops 054c:0ce6) while deep_merge keeps BOTH — confirmed in a direct run of both algorithms. Same applies to [systems.<sys>.pins]. Masked in normal GUI use because set_pins (policy_cmds.py:117-121) and set_backend_template (policy_cmds.py:200-203) re-dump the FULL sub-dict; only a hand-edited/partial local.toml makes the panel + backup view (load_merged) disagree with what the router writes at launch. Silent misreport, no crash; medium is defensible though it leans low-medium given the masking.

confirmed.title: model2.set lets FullScreenWidth/Height be set to an unvalidated raw string, corrupting the resolution
confirmed.description: EDITABLE_KEYS includes the raw INI keys FullScreenWidth and FullScreenHeight (line 83), which are intended to be written ONLY via the synthetic "Resolution" key whose value passes the WxH regex guard (lines 209-222). But model2.set's generic branch (line 224) accepts any key in EDITABLE_KEYS. A request {key:"FullScreenWidth", value:"foo"} therefore passes the guard; _item_by_key("FullScreenWidth") returns None (it is not a GROUPS item), so _coerce_to("string", "foo") returns "foo" verbatim, and _set_key writes `FullScreenWidth=foo` into EMULATOR.INI. The whole point of routing resolution through the synthetic key is the WxH validation, and this back-door bypasses it. More broadly, none of the typed keys are range/bounds-checked at the backend: enum writes str(int(float(value))) with no check against len(options) (e.g. WideScreenWindow=5 is writable), and int/float writes ignore the item's min/max (e.g. GammaR=99.0, RawDevP1=999). The RPC boundary trusts the C++ stepper to stay in range; a malformed or buggy panel request silently writes an out-of-range value the emulator may reject.
confirmed.impact: A malformed/out-of-range request (or a panel bug) writes an invalid value into the shared EMULATOR.INI. A non-numeric FullScreenWidth makes model2.get crash on int(float(w)) and can make m2emu fail to start at native resolution. Self-inflicted only (trusted local panel), but the backend is the validation boundary and does not enforce it.
confirmed.fix: Drop FullScreenWidth/FullScreenHeight from the externally-settable set (keep them only in the internal allow-list used by the Resolution expansion), and in the generic branch validate the value against the item's type bounds before writing: clamp/reject int and float to [min,max], reject enum indices outside range(len(options)).

Two concurrent set_ra_option calls for the same system (e.g. the panel pipelines a bilinear+integer_scale pair, or the user double-taps) can both read the same starting file on different pool threads and the second atomic rename wins, silently dropping the first toggle. confirmed.title: wire-bezels.py writes per-game bezel configs into non-installed RetroArch cores (operator-precedence bug)
confirmed.description: detect_cores() unions detected cores with the hardcoded fallback list. Line 66 reads `if c not in detected and (CFG/c).exists() or c in fallback and c not in detected:`. Python precedence groups this as `(c not in detected and (CFG/c).exists()) or (c in fallback and c not in detected)`. Since the loop iterates `for c in fallback`, the right-hand clause `c in fallback` is ALWAYS true, so the whole condition collapses to `c not in detected` — the `(CFG/c).exists()` check is dead. Every fallback core name is appended whether or not its config dir exists on disk. The docstring/comment explicitly says it should add only fallback dirs 'that exist'.

If the intent is to also keep fallback order regardless of existence, separate the two cases explicitly rather than relying on precedence.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py permanently deletes existing cover when no portrait can be fetched (network-outage data loss)

The download failures at line 134 include ordinary network errors (download() returns False on any exception/timeout) and the case where SGDB has no API key. So a transient outage, a 429, or simply 'no SGDB key configured' causes the only cover the user had to be deleted rather than left alone.

Project rule forbids unrecoverable deletes of media; this is generated media so it's recoverable by re-scraping, hence medium not high.
confirmed.fix: Only delete the landscape cover after confirming a portrait was successfully obtained, OR move the bad cover to a recoverable _TMP/landscape-covers dir with a note instead of unlink. At minimum, skip the 'removed' branch entirely when any download attempt raised a network error (distinguish 'source has no portrait' from 'could not reach source').

confirmed.title: Preview's active Wiimote probe writes to DolphinBar hidraw slots that the wii-nav-bridge holds open and is reading — unguarded cross-process contention
confirmed.description: _devices_wiimotes (preview.route / preview.all path) calls dv.dolphinbar_wiimotes(active=True), which (devices.py:610-649) opens each DolphinBar slot O_RDWR, DRAINS buffered reports, then WRITES a 0x15 status request and reads the reply. Meanwhile wii-nav-bridge.py keeps a NavSlotReader open O_RDWR on the SAME nodes and only pauses on a 'pause' command at game launch (wii-nav-bridge.py:337-339) — it is NOT paused while the MAD panel is just browsing the Preview page. The tester path explicitly guards this (tester_cmds.py:186-187, 1027 return {'probed': False} when a stream is live, plus the wii-tester-slot claim file), but the preview's active probe has NO equivalent guard against the always-running nav bridge. Two readers draining + writing the same hidraw slot concurrently can steal each other's reports, corrupt the report-mode state, and make the bridge's navigation stutter or the probe miscount.
confirmed.impact: Opening the MAD Preview page (or its periodic force-refresh) while Wii Remotes navigate via the bridge can momentarily break Wii-remote menu navigation and/or return a wrong Wiimote count, because the probe's writes/drains race the bridge's reads on the same slot.
confirmed.fix: Make the active probe coordinate with the bridge the same way the tester does: skip active probing (fall back to the passive count or a cached value) when the wii-nav-bridge is live, or have the probe claim the slot via the wii-tester-slot mechanism so the bridge releases it for the probe's duration.

verify.reasoning: devices.py:610-649 _dolphinbar_wiimotes_active opens each slot O_RDWR (line 619), drains buffered reports (623-625), writes 0x15 (629) and reads. device_cmds.py:121 calls this via dv.dolphinbar_wiimotes(active=True) inside devices.wiimotes, which preview.route (preview_cmds.py:144) and preview.all (preview_cmds.py:158) invoke. There is NO _active['stream']/wii-tester-slot guard there, unlike gamepads.list (tester_cmds.py:182-187 returns early when a tester stream is live and uses the slot claim). The wii-nav-bridge (spawned by the fork per README:31) keeps NavSlotReader open and only pauses on game-launch 'pause' (wii-nav-bridge.py:337-339), not while browsing Preview. So the preview probe's drains/writes race the bridge's reads on the same hidraw node. Transient contention (stutter/miscount), not data loss; medium is correct.

confirmed.title: controller-router warning dialog defaults to Cancel (abort launch) on ANY subprocess failure, including a missing/headless DISPLAY
confirmed.description: _show_warning_blocking spawns lib.warning_dialog with DISPLAY=:0 and returns result.returncode; on any exception it logs and returns 1 (Cancel). warning_dialog.show_warning builds a Tk root and mainloops (warning_dialog.py:201). If Tk can't open the display (X not at :0, Wayland-only session, transient X hiccup) the subprocess raises and exits non-zero, or subprocess.run raises -> _show_warning_blocking returns 1 -> _setup returns 1 -> controller-router-wrap.sh sees non-zero and does NOT exec the emulator (wrap.sh:31-34, set -e). So a game that merely has require_sinden (with no gun) or only-X-Arcade-present can be made UNLAUNCHABLE by a display problem, with the only feedback being an ES-DE 'launch failed'. The dialog has an auto-proceed timeout for the normal case, but that only helps when the dialog actually renders.
confirmed.impact: In a degraded display state, a console/lightgun game silently fails to launch (treated as user-cancel) rather than proceeding, with no on-screen explanation — confusing for a non-technical user.
confirmed.fix: On a dialog spawn/exec failure (as opposed to a real user Cancel return of exactly 1), default to PROCEED (return 0) so a broken warning UI never blocks the game; reserve abort for an actual Cancel exit code. Distinguish 'dialog failed to show' from 'user pressed Cancel'.

verify.reasoning: _show_warning_blocking (controller-router.py:96-111) spawns warning_dialog with DISPLAY=:0; on any subprocess exception it returns 1 (line 110-111). warning_dialog.show_warning calls tk.Tk() (warning_dialog.py:80) which raises TclError if the display can't open; that propagates through main()/sys.exit (209-213) and exits the subprocess non-zero. Both the spawn-failure path (returns 1) and a Tk-failure exit (code 1) are indistinguishable from a real user Cancel. _setup returns 1 (controller-router.py:210-212) and wrap.sh (set -euo pipefail, line 14) aborts before exec '$@' (line 31-34). So a degraded display makes a require_sinden/only-X-Arcade console game unlaunchable, reported only as an ES-DE launch failure. Medium is correct.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py rewrites the gamelist via a single non-atomic write_text — a crash mid-write truncates the live gamelist
confirmed.description: After building the full new gamelist text via regex substitution, the file is replaced with a direct GL.write_text(new, encoding='utf-8') (line 252) — not a temp+rename atomic replace (contrast the reference pattern in lib/retroarch_cfg.py and lib/localpolicy.py which do tmp.write + os.replace). A power loss / OOM kill / disk-full between truncate and full write leaves gamelist.xml truncated/partial. A timestamped .bak is written first (line 217) so the ORIGINAL is recoverable, but the live file the user sees can be corrupt.
confirmed.impact: Interrupted run can corrupt the steam gamelist.xml; recoverable only by manually restoring the .bak, which a non-technical user won't know to do.
confirmed.fix: Write to a sibling temp file and os.replace() it onto GL (atomic same-dir rename), matching the project's established atomic-write pattern.

verify.reasoning: steam-fetch-metadata.py:252 is a single `GL.write_text(new, encoding='utf-8')` — truncate+write in place, NOT temp+os.replace. Contrast lib/localpolicy.py:89-92 (tmp.write_text then os.replace) and lib/retroarch_cfg.py:215-217 (tmp.write_text then tmp.replace) which are the project's established atomic pattern. A crash/power-loss/ENOSPC between truncate and full write leaves the live steam gamelist.xml truncated. The timestamped .bak at line 217 makes the ORIGINAL recoverable, which correctly bounds this to medium rather than high.

confirmed.title: Standalone emulator config writers (cemu/xemu/pcsx2/rpcs3/eden) overwrite the live profile non-atomically
confirmed.description: _write_port_from_template and the handheld branch do _port_path(...).write_text(text) / .write_text(tpath.read_text()) directly (cemu_cfg.py:132,203-204); xemu_cfg.py:110, pcsx2_cfg.py:195, rpcs3_cfg.py (open('w')) and eden_cfg.py:187 are the same direct-overwrite pattern. Each takes a ONE-TIME .router-backup before its first write (cemu _backup_once L61-70; xemu L104-106; pcsx2 L184-187; rpcs3 L178-181; eden L181-184), so the user's original is recoverable. But the write itself truncates-then-writes the LIVE controllerN.xml / ini / yml; a crash or power loss mid-write leaves the live profile truncated, and the .router-backup only restores the pre-router-EVER state (the first backup), not the most recent good config. These run at ES-DE game-start with the emulator closed, which is correct by design, but the write is not crash-safe.
confirmed.impact: A power loss / kill during the controller-assignment write (game-start) can truncate the active emulator controller profile; the game then launches with a broken/empty controller config until the user restores the one-time backup.
confirmed.fix: Use the same temp+os.replace atomic write the RetroArch/localpolicy writers use, so the live file is only ever swapped atomically. Keep the one-time backup as the original-recovery escape hatch.

Each has a one-time .router-backup before first write (cemu:61-70, xemu:104-106, eden:181-184, pcsx2:184-187, rpcs3:178-181). The reference atomic writers (retroarch_cfg.py:215-217, localpolicy.py:89-92) use tmp+replace which these do NOT. confirmed.title: steam-fetch-media.py place() unlinks the existing artwork BEFORE copying the new one (non-atomic, no recovery)
confirmed.description: place() loops over existing media for the stem and other.unlink() each (line 94-95) BEFORE shutil.copy2(src_path, dst) (line 97). If the copy then fails (src vanished, ENOSPC, permission), the user's previously-good cover/fanart/marquee is already deleted with nothing written — and it was not moved to _TMP. Note the SAFER pattern in the same file: cdn_download (line 112-114) and sgdb_download (line 133-135) read the new bytes into memory FIRST and only unlink after a successful download, so place() is the odd one out.

confirmed.fix: Copy to a temp name first, then os.replace onto the final path, deleting the old differently-suffixed siblings only AFTER the new file is in place: write dst.with_suffix('.part'), shutil.copy2, then replace + remove stale siblings. Or at minimum copy2 to dst first, then unlink only the OTHER-suffix leftovers.

If the copy fails (src vanished, ENOSPC on the SD card — same /run/media/deck/1tbDeck filesystem as MEDIA, line 43), the existing artwork is already deleted with nothing written and not moved to _TMP (violates the project's never-rm-user-data rule). The same file's cdn_download (109-114) and sgdb_download (130-135) correctly read bytes into memory FIRST and only unlink after success, so place() is the odd one out. confirmed.title: mad_backup.do_restore copies emulator configs back with no emulator/ES-DE running guard (text says 'close emulators' but does not enforce)
confirmed.description: do_restore() shutil.copy2 / shutil.copytree(dirs_exist_ok=True) restores every backup_target (RetroArch config dir, Cemu controllerProfiles, PCSX2/xemu/eden/rpcs3 config) onto the live paths (line 111-115) and the GUI overrides local.toml (line 121). It only returns the advisory string 'Close emulators first if any were open.' (line 130) — there is no actual check. If the target emulator (or ES-DE) is running, it rewrites its config on exit and clobbers the just-restored files; worse, copytree(dirs_exist_ok=True) MERGES rather than replaces, so files present live but absent from the snapshot survive — the restore is not a faithful revert.
confirmed.impact: A user restoring a controller snapshot from the MAD panel while an emulator is still open gets a silent partial/overwritten restore, defeating the safety-net purpose; the merge semantics also leave stray files that can re-introduce the very config they were trying to revert.
confirmed.fix: Before restoring, pgrep the relevant emulators (and ES-DE) and refuse / warn loudly with a hard stop, mirroring dolphin-wii-mode.sh:32-35 and steam-collection-sync.py:83. Document that copytree merges; if a clean replace is intended, move the live dir to _TMP first then copy.

The merge-not-replace and no-guard concerns are real; medium (edge case: user must restore while an emulator is open).

confirmed.title: reorganize-cd-games.py moves user ROMs via os.rename/shutil.move with no recovery manifest and a no-confirm --all

Recoverable in principle (files not deleted) but no record of original locations.

The .__moving window (line 155 then 157) can strand a file on power loss. Files are moved not deleted (recoverable in principle) but with no record of original layout — medium is correct.

confirmed.title: Device name interpolated unescaped into a regex replacement template corrupts Cemu controller XML
confirmed.description: `text = _DISPLAY_RE.sub(rf"\g<1>{dev.name}\g<3>", text, count=1)` interpolates `dev.name` (the raw evdev/USB product string, externally controlled by whatever pad is plugged in) directly into a `re.sub` REPLACEMENT TEMPLATE. In a replacement template, backslash sequences are interpreted as backreferences: I verified that a pad named `Weird \g<1> Name` produces corrupted, tag-duplicated XML (`<display_name>Weird <display_name> Name...`), and a name with an invalid group ref (e.g. trailing `\` or `\9`) raises re.error and aborts the whole cemu assign() mid-launch. Separately, the name is written into XML CONTENT with no XML-escaping, so a name containing `&`, `<`, or `>` (common on knockoff / renamed-Bluetooth pads) yields invalid XML. Note the sibling line 130 (`new_uuid`) is safe because the value is hex+digit, and model2_cmds._set_key avoids this entirely by using a lambda instead of a template string — so the fix pattern already exists in the repo.
confirmed.impact: A Wii U game launched with a pad whose name contains `&`/`<`/`>`/`\` gets a silently corrupted controllerN.xml: Cemu can't load the profile so that controller doesn't work, and in the `\`+invalid-group case the Python exception aborts cemu routing for the launch. Common pads are unaffected; oddly/maliciously named ones break.
confirmed.fix: Don't interpolate into a replacement template. Use a function replacement so the value is treated literally, and XML-escape the name first, e.g.: `from xml.sax.saxutils import escape` then `text = _DISPLAY_RE.sub(lambda m: m.group(1) + escape(dev.name) + m.group(3), text, count=1)`. Apply the same literal-replacement approach anywhere device names reach re.sub.

verify.reasoning: cemu_cfg.py:131 `text = _DISPLAY_RE.sub(rf"\g<1>{dev.name}\g<3>", text, count=1)` interpolates dev.name into a re.sub REPLACEMENT template. dev.name is the raw evdev.InputDevice.name (confirmed lib/devices.py:49 'evdev kernel name'), externally controlled by the plugged pad. I reproduced: name 'Weird \g<1> Name' -> '<display_name>Weird <display_name> Name</display_name>' (tag-duplicated corrupt XML); name 'Pad \9' -> re.error 'invalid group reference 9' which would abort assign() mid-launch; '&'/'<'/'>' names pass through unescaped into XML content. assign() reaches line 131 via _write_port_from_template on the normal external-pad path (cemu_cfg.py:233-234). Sibling line 130 (new_uuid) is safe (hex/digit); model2_cmds._set_key (model2_cmds.py:107) already uses the lambda fix pattern. Medium is fair — only odd/maliciously named pads break.

confirmed.title: Cover/media files deleted outright instead of moved to recoverable _TMP

These are scraped/re-fetchable media rather than save data, so impact is lower, but the line-143 'removed' branch destroys a file the tool cannot reproduce (Steam had no portrait) with no recovery path.

confirmed.fix: Move deleted media to a timestamped _TMP dir (same filesystem as MEDIA, e.g. /run/media/deck/1tbDeck/_TMP_steam-cover-<ts>) with a RECOVERY.txt instead of unlink(), per project rule #5 — or at minimum keep the existing landscape cover when no portrait is available rather than deleting it.

This substantially overlaps finding 11.4 (same line-143/145 'removed' branch) — both are confirmed; if deduplicating, treat 11.4 and 12.3 as the same root issue plus 12.3's added line-138 replacement-delete and the steam-fetch-media.py instances. Re-fetchable media lowers impact, but the line-145 branch destroys an unreproducible file. Medium is correct.

confirmed.title: Base controller-policy.toml parsed with no error handling — a typo aborts every game launch
confirmed.description: load_policy() parses the human-edited base policy with `base = tomllib.load(f)` inside no try/except (contrast the LOCAL file at lines 51-56, which catches TOMLDecodeError/OSError and degrades gracefully). controller-router.py runs load_policy() synchronously on every emulator launch via the ES-DE game-start hook; a single hand-edit typo in controller-policy.toml therefore raises TOMLDecodeError and crashes the router, which (via controller-router-wrap.sh translating the non-zero exit) blocks the emulator from launching — for ALL systems, with no on-screen feedback beyond the router log.
confirmed.impact: One stray character in controller-policy.toml makes every routed game fail to launch with a generic ES-DE error and no obvious cause; the user (non-technical) cannot tell the policy file is at fault.
confirmed.fix: Wrap the base parse in try/except TOMLDecodeError and on failure log the parse error to stderr/router.log and fall back to a minimal safe default (e.g. `{'systems': {}}`), so a typo degrades to 'no routing' instead of 'no launches'. Optionally surface it via the blocking warning_dialog so the user sees why.

verify.reasoning: routing.py:48-50 `if POLICY_FILE.is_file(): with POLICY_FILE.open('rb') as f: base = tomllib.load(f)` has NO try/except, in direct contrast to the LOCAL file at lines 51-56 which catches `(tomllib.TOMLDecodeError, OSError)` with the comment 'a broken local file must never break routing'. load_policy() is the router's real resolution entry (per the module docstring lines 1-13), run on game launch. A typo in the human-edited controller-policy.toml raises TOMLDecodeError and crashes the router. I could not in this session confirm controller-router-wrap.sh translates that to a launch block, so the 'blocks every launch' impact is plausible but the wrap-script propagation is not verified here — the asymmetric missing guard itself is definitively real. Medium is fair.

confirmed.title: openbor-fetch-media.py crashes on a manifest missing a DIR= line (unguarded .group)
confirmed.description: appid_for() does `dirn=re.search(r'^DIR=(.*)$', man, re.M).group(1)` with no None-check, even though the immediately preceding PREFIX= lookup (lines 68-71) correctly guards with `if m:`. A `.openbor` manifest that has a PREFIX that isn't all-digits but no DIR= line (or no DIR= at all) makes re.search return None and `.group(1)` raise AttributeError. This propagates up through copy_art() (line 87, no try) into main()'s loop and aborts the whole art pass.
confirmed.impact: A single malformed/incomplete .openbor manifest crashes the entire OpenBOR media fetch instead of skipping that one game. It's a manually-run utility, so no daemon dies, but the run fails wholesale.
confirmed.fix: Guard it: `dm = re.search(r'^DIR=(.*)$', man, re.M); dirn = dm.group(1) if dm else ''` and skip the vdf-matching loop when dirn is empty, returning None for that stem.

verify.reasoning: openbor-fetch-media.py:72 `dirn=re.search(r'^DIR=(.*)$', man, re.M).group(1)` has no None-check, while the immediately preceding PREFIX lookup at lines 68-71 correctly guards with `if m:`. main() line 182-183 calls copy_art(stem) in a bare loop (no try/except), and copy_art line 86 calls appid_for unguarded. A .openbor manifest with a missing/non-digit PREFIX AND no DIR= line raises AttributeError and aborts the whole art pass. Note the crash is narrower than 'any missing DIR=': if PREFIX is all-digits the function returns at line 71 before reaching 72. Still reachable for incomplete manifests; medium/robustness correct for a manually-run utility.

confirmed.title: controller-router-wrap.sh silently succeeds (launches nothing) when the emulator command after '--' is empty
confirmed.description: The arg-count guard requires `$# -ge 5` (SYSTEM ROM NAME FULLNAME --), but that is satisfied by a trailing bare `--` with no emulator command. After `shift 4` and the `--` shift, `$@` is empty, and `exec "$@"` with zero args is a no-op in bash: execution falls through and the script exits 0. Verified in a bash repro. ES-DE then thinks the launch succeeded though nothing ran.
confirmed.impact: A malformed es_systems.xml <command> (everything up to and including '--' but no emulator) runs the router setup, writes overrides, then exits 0 having launched nothing — looks like an instant return-to-ES-DE with no error. Only reachable via a broken systems config, not a normal launch.
confirmed.fix: After the final shift, assert there is a command: `[[ $# -ge 1 ]] || { echo "...: no emulator command after --" >&2; exit 2; }` before `exec "$@"`.

confirmed.title: set_system_flag computes the 'revert' default from the system's own base entry without resolving inherits

It becomes wrong the moment a child inherits a parent that overrides a default the other way — e.g. mugen/openbor set warn_when_only_xarcade=false on themselves; a future [systems.foo] inherits='mugen' would get heuristic default True but router-effective base False. confirmed.title: Two tomllib.load() calls leak the underlying file object (never closed)

confirmed.title: Nav-sound playback leaks zombie child processes for the GUI's lifetime
confirmed.description: Sound.play() launches the audio player (pw-play / paplay / aplay) with subprocess.Popen and never keeps a reference, never calls .wait()/.poll(), and the app installs no SIGCHLD/SIG_IGN handler (confirmed: no SIGCHLD handling anywhere in router-config-gui.py or gui_sound.py). Each played sound therefore becomes a zombie <defunct> process once the short-lived player exits, and they accumulate. The 0.11s nav debounce (_NAV_DEBOUNCE) caps the rate but does not prevent accumulation over a long session with heavy menu navigation (every focus move, select, back, and section change plays a sound).
confirmed.impact: A long MAD session accumulates many defunct child processes (one per sound played). Practically harmless on a desktop but untidy and could exhaust the PID table in an extreme session; it is a genuine, unbounded resource leak.
confirmed.fix: Install signal.signal(signal.SIGCHLD, signal.SIG_IGN) once at module/app init (auto-reaps children on Linux), or reap opportunistically (keep the Popen handles in a small list and poll() them on the next play), or pass them to a periodic reaper. SIG_IGN is the simplest fix for fire-and-forget players.

verify.reasoning: gui_sound.py:104-108 launches the player via subprocess.Popen and never stores the handle, never calls wait()/poll(). grep confirms the only signal handling in the app is faulthandler for SIGTERM/SIGHUP/SIGINT/SIGSEGV-class (router-config-gui.py:75) — no SIGCHLD/SIG_IGN anywhere. Each played sound becomes a defunct child after the short-lived player exits; they accumulate over a session. The 0.11s _NAV_DEBOUNCE (line 98) only rate-limits 'nav' events, not select/back/launch. Real unbounded resource leak; correctly rated low (practically harmless on a desktop).

confirmed.title: Camera-preview ffmpeg stderr log file descriptor is leaked on every preview start
confirmed.description: _cam_preview() passes stderr=open(logdir / "sinden-preview.log", "ab") inline to subprocess.Popen. The sibling helper _run() (line 1973) does this correctly with a 'with open(...) as lf:' block that closes the parent fd right after spawn (its comment even says 'closed in parent after spawn'). The sibling _run() (1973) does it correctly with 'with open(...) as lf:' (comment 'closed in parent after spawn'). One leaked fd per Preview press, held until process exit. confirmed.title: Cemu display_name/uuid substitution can corrupt XML or crash on device names with regex-replacement metachars
confirmed.description: _write_port_from_template interpolates the live device name directly into a regex REPLACEMENT string: `_DISPLAY_RE.sub(rf"\g<1>{dev.name}\g<3>", text, count=1)` (line 131) and similarly the uuid at line 130. In a replacement string, backslash sequences are special, so a `dev.name` containing `\g<...>` raises IndexError ('unknown group name') and a `\1`-style sequence silently injects garbage — verified: name `'8BitDo \g<bad>'` raises IndexError, name `'Pad \1 thing'` produces `'<display_name><display_name> thing</display_name>'`. Additionally dev.name is written verbatim into XML with no escaping, so a name containing `&`/`<`/`>` yields invalid XML in <display_name>. An exception here aborts that port's write inside assign() (uncaught), and the kernel evdev name is attacker-influenceable for a BT pad with a crafted name.

confirmed.fix: Use a function replacement to avoid backreference interpretation, e.g. `_DISPLAY_RE.sub(lambda m: m.group(1)+xml_escape(dev.name)+m.group(3), text, count=1)` and the same for the uuid sub; XML-escape dev.name with xml.sax.saxutils.escape before insertion.

The uuid sub at line 130 uses new_uuid built from numeric index + hex guid, so that one is safe. dev.name is the kernel evdev name (BT-pad influenceable). Exception is uncaught in assign(). confirmed.title: es_collections lru_cache is process-lifetime but mad-backend is a long-lived daemon — stale collection list across a panel session

confirmed.title: esde-splash-gen.sh silently deletes splash.svg if the tomllib parse transiently fails on a valid config

If that subprocess fails for any transient reason (e.g. tomllib import hiccup, momentary read error) `cfg` is empty, so `mode` is empty, falls to `[ -z "$mode" ] && mode=off`, and the `off|*` case runs `rm -f "$OUT"` (line 104), wiping ~/ES-DE/resources/graphics/splash.svg even when the user had configured fixed_image/random_image with a perfectly valid local.toml. So a transient parse failure on a valid fixed/random config is treated identically to mode=off and wipes the override. confirmed.title: wii-monitor.py busy-loops and never drops a slot on remote-disconnect EOF
confirmed.description: In the read loop, a select-ready fd that returns an empty buffer (os.read returns b'' = EOF, which happens when a Wii Remote disconnects / the hidraw node goes away without raising OSError) hits `if not buf or len(buf) < 3: continue` on line 130. This skips line 131 (`st['last_frame'] = now`) and never closes/pops the fd. Because an EOF fd stays perpetually 'ready' in select(), the outer while-loop spins at full speed on that fd, and the keepalive block (line 156-158) keeps firing set_mode() writes on the dead descriptor (which then fail silently in w()). The fd is only ever reclaimed on an OSError from os.read (line 128), not on a clean EOF.
confirmed.impact: When a remote drops, wii-monitor pins a CPU core busy-looping and leaks the slot fd until os.read happens to raise. This is a standalone diagnostic tool (logs to ~/wii-monitor.log, Ctrl-C to stop), not on any game-launch path, so user impact is limited to the operator running it manually. WiiSlotReader (the production reader) correctly handles the same case via _on_lost() on `if not buf`.
confirmed.fix: Treat empty buf as a disconnect, mirroring WiiSlotReader._pump: `if not buf: log(drop); os.close(fd); slots.pop(fd); break` and only `continue` for the `len(buf) < 3` short-frame case (and still bump last_frame there so keepalive timing stays sane).

verify.reasoning: wii-monitor.py:130 `if not buf or len(buf) < 3: continue` skips the close/pop and last_frame bump; only an OSError at line 128 reclaims the fd. A clean EOF (b'') from a disconnected remote leaves the fd perpetually select-ready (line 123), so the while-loop spins and the keepalive at lines 156-158 keeps firing set_mode() on the dead fd. The production reader lib/wii_slot_reader.py:130-131 correctly does `if not buf: self._on_lost(); return`, confirming the finder's contrast. Standalone diagnostic tool (header lines 7-8: logs to ~/wii-monitor.log, Ctrl-C to stop), not on a launch path, so low is correct.

confirmed.title: daphne.bind / _dp_bind_press leak proc on communicate() timeout without reaping
confirmed.description: _bind runs hypseus_capture.py with `proc.communicate(timeout=14)`. The capture subprocess itself has a 10 s SDL timeout, so 14 s should normally suffice, but if communicate() raises TimeoutExpired it is caught by the broad `except Exception` and `proc.kill()` is called — without a following proc.wait()/communicate() to reap the killed child. The Tk twin (lib/mad_daphne_page.py:289-300 _dp_bind_press worker) has the identical pattern. Per the subprocess docs, after kill() on a timed-out communicate you must call communicate()/wait() again or the process can linger as a zombie and its stdout pipe fd is not closed.
confirmed.impact: On the rare path where the capture subprocess hangs past 14 s (e.g. SDL stuck on a wedged joystick), a zombie child and a leaked pipe fd accumulate per bind attempt. Not data-affecting and self-limited (capture has its own 10 s deadline), but it is a latent leak in a daemon meant to be long-lived.
confirmed.fix: In the except branch after proc.kill(), call `try: proc.communicate(timeout=2) except Exception: pass` (or proc.wait(timeout=...)) to reap the child and close the pipes; apply the same to lib/mad_daphne_page.py.

verify.reasoning: daphne_cmds.py:198-205: `except Exception` calls proc.kill() (line 201) and the finally (lines 204-205) only flips input.lock — there is no proc.wait()/communicate() to reap the killed child or close the stdout PIPE (opened at line 191). The Tk twin mad_daphne_page.py:294-300 has the identical kill-without-reap. Per subprocess docs this can leave a zombie + leaked pipe fd on the rare path where capture hangs past 14s. Latent leak in a long-lived daemon; self-limited by the capture's own 10s timeout, so low is correct.

confirmed.title: singe-indexer.sh framefile path parsing truncates at first space

confirmed.title: sinden-smoother-tune.sh drops the snap_threshold key on save, resetting it to the daemon default

confirmed.title: camera.set RPC does not validate player ∈ {1,2} before indexing — KeyError on bad input

confirmed.title: supermodel-proton.sh restore() trap: `&&...||` precedence can skip the X-Arcade reattach
confirmed.description: restore() (the EXIT/INT/TERM trap) chains `[ -n "$XARCADE_PTR_SLAVE" ] && [ -n "$CORE_PTR" ] && xinput reattach ... || true` on line 64. With && / || left-to-right precedence, if XARCADE_PTR_SLAVE or CORE_PTR is empty the left side is false and the `|| true` swallows it — fine. But the pattern is fragile: the `|| true` only guards the FIRST failing test, and the subsequent two restore lines (65, 66) are separate statements, so a non-zero xinput reattach on line 64 is masked but the float on the X-Arcade pointer applied earlier (line 59) is correctly reverted only if XARCADE_PTR_SLAVE was captured. The net effect under `set -uo pipefail` (no -e) is benign, but it means a failed reattach is silently ignored and the X-Arcade trackball can be left floated after exit if the IDs shifted while the emulator ran.
confirmed.impact: Edge case: if device xinput IDs change during the Supermodel session, the X-Arcade pointer may be left detached/floated after exit, so its trackball stops moving the desktop cursor until re-plugged or the next sinden-start. No data loss.
confirmed.fix: Use explicit if-blocks per device in restore(), and re-resolve slave IDs at restore time rather than trusting the launch-time IDs.

verify.reasoning: supermodel-proton.sh:12 sets `set -uo pipefail` (no -e). The restore() trap (line 64) uses `[ -n A ] && [ -n B ] && xinput reattach ... || true`; shell left-associativity makes this `(((A && B) && reattach) || true)`, so the `|| true` DOES swallow a failed reattach — the finding's claim that `|| true` only guards the first test is imprecise. But the finding's rated conclusion is correct and low-severity: a failed reattach is silently ignored, so if device xinput IDs shift while the emulator runs, the X-Arcade pointer floated at line 59 may be left detached after exit. No data loss. Confirmed as a real low/edge robustness gap; reasoning about precedence is partly muddled but the outcome stands.

confirmed.title: sinden-camera.sh edits the user's tuned LightgunMono.exe.config via `sed -i` with no backup and unescaped replacement

confirmed.title: reorganize-cd-games.py can leave a renamed game file orphaned as '<name>.__moving' if mkdir fails mid-operation
confirmed.description: When the target folder name collides with the .cue/.m3u file itself, the script does `os.rename(target, tmp_target)` (file -> '<name>.__moving'), then `os.makedirs(target)`, then `os.rename(tmp_target, target/<name>)`. If makedirs or the second rename fails (permission, disk full, name clash), the original launchable file is left on disk renamed to '<name>.__moving' with no folder and no recovery note — ES-DE would no longer see the game and the user has no breadcrumb explaining the odd filename.

If makedirs or the second rename fails mid-window, the launchable file is orphaned as '<name>.__moving' with no folder and no recovery note. Files aren't lost; narrow failure window; low is correct.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py rebuild_block silently drops gamelist tags that aren't simple <word>...</word> pairs
