confirmed.title: devices.unwatch / devices.watch have an unsynchronized token swap that can leak a hotplug watcher thread or drop a live one
confirmed.description: _WATCH = {"token": None} is read/written by devices.watch and devices.unwatch with NO lock, while every other RPC module that shares mutable state across the stdin thread + worker pool uses an explicit lock (capture _LOCK, camera _CAM_LOCK, sizes _SIZES_LOCK). devices.watch is a FAST (inline) method and devices.unwatch is FAST too, so both run on the stdin thread and are serialized in practice — but _devices_unwatch (line 160-163) does `tok = _WATCH.pop('token', None); _WATCH['token'] = None` which is a redundant pop-then-reset, and devices.watch (line 151-156) checks/sets without coordinating with the WatchStream's own cleanup (the stream removes itself from _STREAMS on exit but never clears _WATCH['token']). If the watch stream ever ends on its own (e.g. an exception path) _WATCH['token'] still points at a dead token, so the next devices.watch returns {'already': True} with a stale token and the hotplug push silently never resumes.
confirmed.impact: Edge case: if the device-watch stream dies unexpectedly, the panel believes a watcher is still running (stale token) and stops receiving devices.changed hotplug events for the rest of the session.
confirmed.fix: Clear _WATCH['token'] in the stream's cleanup() (like the tester/camera streams null their _active/_cam entries), and guard the dict with a lock for consistency with the other command modules.

verify.reasoning: device_cmds.py:147 _WATCH = {'token': None}; both devices.watch (150-156) and devices.unwatch (159-163) are registered FAST (no slow=True) so they serialize on the stdin thread — the lock is not strictly needed, matching the finding's own caveat. The real defect: _WatchStream (128-144) has no cleanup() override, and the base Stream._guarded (rpc.py:119-126) only pops itself from _STREAMS, never clearing _WATCH['token']. If the watch stream dies on its own, _WATCH['token'] stays set, so the next devices.watch returns {'already': True} with a stale token and hotplug events never resume. Edge case; low is correct.

confirmed.title: quit-combo-watcher / wiimote-quit-watcher run a policy-derived quit_cmd via shell=True
confirmed.description: _quit() does subprocess.run(quit_cmd, shell=True) where quit_cmd is the per-launch --quit-cmd string, ultimately sourced from es_systems.quit_cmd(system, policy) (controller-router.py:467-475) i.e. derived from controller-policy.toml + the system definition. It is also string-mangled (quit_cmd.replace('-TERM','-KILL')) and embedded into another shell string for the setsid backstop (quit-combo-watcher.py:111-116). wiimote-quit-watcher.py:119 has the identical subprocess.run(QUITCMD, shell=True) pattern (QUITCMD from env/default). Because the whole launch seam passes these commands through the shell, a system whose quit_cmd or whose name (it can flow into the command) contains shell metacharacters would be word-split / expanded / command-substituted at quit time.
confirmed.impact: A malformed or hostile system quit_cmd in policy is executed verbatim by the shell during a game session; in the normal case the strings are author-controlled so this is low-likelihood, but it is an unvalidated shell-exec on a launch path and a foot-gun for any future policy edit (e.g. a path with spaces or a '$' silently breaks the quit).
confirmed.fix: Where the command is a simple kill/pkill, build an argv list and run without shell=True; if a shell is genuinely needed, pass the command via a fixed argv (['bash','-c', cmd]) AND validate/whitelist the quit_cmd shape when loading policy. The .replace('-TERM','-KILL') string surgery is also fragile — derive the kill variant structurally rather than by substring.

verify.reasoning: quit-combo-watcher.py:120 runs `subprocess.run(quit_cmd, shell=True)` and line 111 does string surgery `quit_cmd.replace('-TERM','-KILL')` then embeds it into another shell string at line 113 (`f"trap '' TERM; sleep ...; {kill_cmd}"`) executed via setsid bash -c (115). wiimote-quit-watcher.py:119 has the identical `subprocess.run(QUITCMD, shell=True)` (QUITCMD from env/default at :28). So shell=True on a launch-path command is real. HOWEVER the input trust is higher than claimed: the curated path returns `str(be['quit_cmd'])` verbatim from controller-policy.toml (es_systems.py:170) which is author-controlled, and the DERIVED path (_derive_quit, es_systems.py:126-155) sanitizes the token via `re.split(r'[^A-Za-z0-9]', ...)` (lines 149/152) so no shell metacharacters survive and rejects names <3 chars. The 'system name flows into the command with metachars' claim is thus refuted for the derived path. This is a genuine shell-exec foot-gun + fragile substring surgery (the .replace breaks if a quit_cmd doesn't literally contain '-TERM'), but not exploitable on any normal/derived path. Downgrading from medium to low: it's a hardening/robustness note, not a real bug hit in normal use.

confirmed.title: WiiTesterStream STOP->START restart can leak the previous tester's hidraw slot reader (cleanup runs after the new run() already claimed it)
confirmed.description: tester.start (line 985-987) stops the previous stream then sleeps only 0.2s before constructing the new one; stop_stream just sets the Event and does NOT join (rpc.py:138-144). The WiiTesterStream's cleanup (line 963-977) is what calls reader.stop(timeout=0.6) and removes the wii-tester-slot claim, and it runs on the OLD stream's thread AFTER the run loop unwinds. The comment at line 967-969 acknowledges 'this cleanup runs AFTER the new run()'. So on a fast STOP->START on the SAME slot: the new WiiTesterStream.run() writes the slot claim and constructs a fresh NavSlotReader/WiiSlotReader on the node while the OLD reader is still being torn down — two WiiSlotReader daemon threads can briefly hold the same hidraw node O_RDWR and write conflicting report-mode bytes, and if the old cleanup's slot-file ownership check (line 971-973) races the new claim write it can either no-op (good) or, on a different node, unlink a claim the new stream just wrote.
confirmed.impact: Rapidly restarting the Wii tester (or switching slots quickly) can momentarily run two readers on one DolphinBar slot, producing a flickering/garbled tester display and, in the worst case, dropping the slot claim so the nav bridge re-grabs a slot under test.
confirmed.fix: In tester.start, join the previous stream's thread (bounded timeout) before constructing the replacement, instead of a fixed time.sleep(0.2); the Stream already exposes _thread. This guarantees the old reader's cleanup (ungrab/close + claim removal) completes before the new claim/open.

verify.reasoning: Verified the race: rpc.py stop_stream (138-144) only does `s.stopped.set()` and returns — it does NOT join (confirmed; only stop_all_streams at 147-155 joins). tester.start (tester_cmds.py:985-987) calls stop_stream then `time.sleep(0.2)` (no join) before constructing the new stream and writing _active['stream'] (997). WiiTesterStream.run writes the slot claim atomically (882-884) and constructs+starts WiiSlotReader (891-892) early in run(); its cleanup (963-977) calls reader.stop(timeout=0.6) and the slot-file ownership unlink on the OLD thread, acknowledged by the comment 'this cleanup runs AFTER the new run()' (967-969). So on a fast same-slot STOP->START the old reader can still be alive while the new reader opens the node. The ownership check at 971-973 guards the same-node unlink correctly, but the two-readers-on-one-node window is real. Low severity is appropriate (transient flicker, daemon-life only on worst case).

confirmed.title: RunFullStream releases _RUN_ACTIVE in run()'s finally even when start() failed — relies on start() exception path to compensate, fragile invariant
confirmed.description: backup.run_full acquires _RUN_ACTIVE (line 181), then RunFullStream(argv).start() launches the thread whose run() finally ALWAYS does _RUN_ACTIVE.release() (line 154). The acquire and the release thus live in different objects/threads, coordinated only by the comment 'start() never ran run()'s finally' and the except clause that releases on a start() failure (line 190). sinden.install reuses the SAME lock through the same class (sinden_cmds.py:119-126). If start() ever partially starts the thread but then raises (or run() raises before reaching its try), the lock accounting can desync: a release without a matching acquire on a plain threading.Lock raises RuntimeError, or the lock is never released and all future backups/installs return EBUSY for the daemon's life. The current code is correct on the happy path but the acquire/release split across two methods and two threads is a latent single-flight-lock hazard, especially as a second caller (sinden.install) now shares it.
confirmed.impact: A future change or an unusual exception in stream startup can either wedge the backup/install single-flight lock permanently (every run -> EBUSY until the panel is restarted) or raise a spurious RuntimeError on over-release.
confirmed.fix: Make the lock ownership explicit and self-contained: acquire inside run() (or pass an already-acquired flag the stream owns), and ensure exactly one acquire pairs with the run()-finally release; alternatively use a bounded try/finally entirely within the @method so the lock lifetime doesn't straddle thread boundaries.

verify.reasoning: Verified: _RUN_ACTIVE = threading.Lock() (backup_cmds.py:46). backup.run_full acquires non-blocking at 181, RunFullStream(argv).start() at 188, and run()'s finally ALWAYS releases at 154; the start()-failure except releases at 190 with comment 'start() never ran run()'s finally'. sinden.install reuses the SAME lock through the same class (sinden_cmds.py:119-125). The acquire/release straddle two methods and two threads — a latent single-flight hazard. In practice run() begins `rc=-1; try:` so once the thread runs the finally is guaranteed, and start() (rpc.py:110-112) only does thread.start() so the failure window is narrow; the code is correct on the happy path. Low severity is right — it's a latent fragility, not a current bug.

confirmed.title: X-Arcade tester opens nodes then grabs in a loop; a grab failure after opening earlier nodes is handled, but a partial-open with zero opened leaves no input.lock and silently no-ops
confirmed.description: XArcadeTesterStream.run opens each candidate node, set_blocking, and grabs the gamepad nodes (line 758-777); on a per-node grab failure it increments `failed`, closes that node, and continues (line 768-773). If NO node opens (`opened` empty) it emits no_device and returns (line 778-781) — but it returns BEFORE setting self.locked / emitting input.lock, which is correct. The subtler gap: cleanup (line 844-857) iterates self.devs which is only assigned at line 802 AFTER the open loop; if run() raises between opening nodes (line 758-777, devices opened/grabbed) and the self.devs=opened assignment (line 802), _guarded's finally calls cleanup() but self.devs is still [] from __init__, so the already-grabbed/opened nodes are NEVER ungrabbed/closed — leaking an EVIOCGRAB on the X-Arcade until the daemon exits.
confirmed.impact: An exception in the P1/P2 tagging block (usb_iface_num / sorting, lines 784-801) after pads are grabbed would leak the grab on the X-Arcade gamepad nodes, leaving the cab's player sides dead in-game until the backend is restarted.
confirmed.fix: Assign self.devs = opened immediately after the open loop (before the tagging block), or wrap the open results in self.devs as you go, so cleanup() always sees every opened/grabbed node regardless of where run() fails.

verify.reasoning: Verified: XArcadeTesterStream.__init__ sets self.devs=[] (tester_cmds.py:657). run() opens+grabs into a LOCAL `opened` list (757-777) and only assigns self.devs=opened at 802, AFTER the P1/P2 tagging block (784-801) which imports usb_iface_num and sorts via lambdas. cleanup() (844-857) iterates self.devs. If run() raises between the grab loop and line 802 (e.g. in the import at 784 or the sort at 795), _guarded's finally (rpc.py:119-123) calls cleanup() while self.devs is still [], leaking the EVIOCGRAB on the X-Arcade nodes until daemon exit. Real but low-likelihood (tagging block is simple); low severity is correct.

confirmed.title: _ENUM_CACHE module global is mutated by the watch thread and inline router/RPC calls with no lock
confirmed.description: enumerate_devices() reads and mutates the module-global _ENUM_CACHE dict (populate at line 193, evict stale entries at line 216-217) with no lock. In the daemon, enumerate_devices is called from the stdin thread (devices.scan), the worker pool (gamepads.list, preview.*, capture identify), and the device-watch stream thread (_WatchStream.run -> _scan at device_cmds.py:141), concurrently. CPython's GIL makes individual dict ops atomic, but the read-modify-evict sequence (build `alive`, then `for p in [p for p in _ENUM_CACHE if p not in alive]: del _ENUM_CACHE[p]`) is not atomic across threads: a concurrent walk can add a path between the comprehension snapshot and the del, or two walks can both try to del the same evicted key (KeyError is avoided by the comprehension snapshot, but a freshly-added entry from thread B can be evicted by thread A's stale-eviction). Worst case is a redundant re-probe (cost, not correctness) or a transient missing/duplicate entry in one walk's output.
confirmed.impact: Under concurrent enumeration (panel idle-watch + a preview/route at the same time) a device can be momentarily mis-probed or evicted-then-reprobed, causing a one-off wrong/empty row or extra ~37ms close cost; not a hard crash given the GIL, but the cache's RA-order invariant is theoretically violable.
confirmed.fix: Guard _ENUM_CACHE access with a small module lock (the file already uses _SDL_LOCK as precedent), or build a fresh local dict per call and swap it in atomically at the end.

verify.reasoning: Verified: _ENUM_CACHE: dict is a module global (devices.py:134) read at 163, written at 171/193, and bulk-evicted at 216-217 (`for p in [p for p in _ENUM_CACHE if p not in alive]: del _ENUM_CACHE[p]`) with NO lock. The file has _SDL_LOCK precedent (394). enumerate_devices is reachable concurrently: devices.scan runs inline on the stdin thread (device_cmds.py:67-69, fast), slow=True methods (devices.sdl:72-76 etc.) run on a 4-worker ThreadPoolExecutor (rpc.py:24), and _WatchStream.run (device_cmds.py:132-144) calls _scan() on its own thread. The GIL keeps individual ops atomic so no hard crash, but the read-modify-evict sequence isn't atomic — a freshly-added entry can be evicted by another walk, causing a redundant re-probe or one-off wrong/empty row. Low severity (robustness, not correctness on any reliable path) is correct.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py MEDIA dir is hardcoded to /run/media/deck/1tbDeck — silently mis-targets on a differently-named card
confirmed.description: MEDIA = Path('/run/media/deck/1tbDeck/downloaded_media/steam') is hardcoded (line 38). deck-backup.sh:57-59 deliberately AVOIDS baking in the card volume name ('it changes if the user swaps cards') and discovers downloaded_media dynamically. Here, if the SD card mounts under a different label, MEDIA points at a path that mkdir -p will happily CREATE under a stale/empty mountpoint, so covers/screenshots get written to a dead location while the real media dir is untouched — and fix_cover's unlink operates on the wrong (empty) dir.
confirmed.impact: On a card swap / re-image the script writes art to a phantom directory and ES-DE never sees it; the user sees no covers and no error.
confirmed.fix: Resolve the media root the way deck-backup.sh does (read MediaDirectory from es_settings.xml, fall back to ls -d /run/media/deck/*/downloaded_media) instead of the hardcoded card name.

verify.reasoning: steam-fetch-metadata.py:38 hardcodes `MEDIA = Path('/run/media/deck/1tbDeck/downloaded_media/steam')`, and main() line 199-200 does `(MEDIA / sub).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` which will create the path under any stale mountpoint. deck-backup.sh:56-60 deliberately discovers the media root dynamically (reads MediaDirectory from es_settings.xml at line 56, falls back to `ls -d /run/media/deck/*/downloaded_media` at line 59) precisely because the card label changes on swap. On a differently-labelled card, art is written to a phantom dir with no error. Low severity is appropriate (card-swap edge case, no data loss).

confirmed.title: daphne.build_index passes an RPC-supplied arg as a ROM path segment (path traversal)
confirmed.description: daphne.build_index takes `arg = params.get('arg', 'all')` straight from the RPC request and passes it to `_detached([HERE / 'singe-indexer.sh', arg], ...)`, which runs `index_game "$ROMS/$arg"` (singe-indexer.sh:153). Because _detached uses Popen with an argv list (no shell), there is no command injection, but `arg` is unvalidated and is concatenated into a filesystem path inside the script, so a value like `../../something` resolves outside ~/ROMs/daphne. The arg is normally one of the GUI's enumerated game-dir basenames, so this is only reachable by a malformed/crafted RPC, and the script only reads/launches Hypseus against the dir (it does not delete it). Worth tightening as defense-in-depth.
confirmed.impact: A crafted or buggy RPC could point the indexer at an arbitrary directory; in normal GUI use the arg is a trusted basename so impact is minimal. No injection or deletion.
confirmed.fix: Validate `arg` against the enumerated game basenames (the same list _games() returns) or reject path separators / leading dots before passing it on; or require it to match one of DAPHNE_ROOT's *.daphne/*.singe dir stems plus the literal 'all'.

verify.reasoning: daphne_cmds.py:281-282 `arg = params.get('arg', 'all'); _detached([HERE / 'singe-indexer.sh', arg], ...)` passes the RPC-supplied arg unvalidated into singe-indexer.sh:153 `index_game "$ROMS/$arg"`, where it is concatenated into a filesystem path. _detached (sinden_cmds.py:56) uses Popen with an argv list (no shell), so there is NO command injection — the candidate correctly states this. index_game (singe-indexer.sh:51-142) only reads framefiles and launches Hypseus against the dir (writes .dat files into it via the indexer; no rm of the game dir — the only rm at lines 116/141 deletes the generated $WORK script). So '../../x' could point the indexer outside ~/ROMs/daphne, but only in a crafted/buggy RPC; normal GUI use passes a trusted _games() basename. Low/defense-in-depth is the right rating.

confirmed.title: _quit backstop derives the KILL command by blind string replace of '-TERM'->'-KILL'
confirmed.description: kill_cmd = quit_cmd.replace('-TERM', '-KILL') is a substring replace over the whole command string. It only fires the backstop when '-TERM' appears (guarded at line 112), which is correct for the curated backends, but it would also rewrite any incidental '-TERM' substring (e.g. a path/arg containing '-TERM...') and, for quit_cmds that ALREADY contain an explicit `pkill -KILL` escalation (eden controller-policy.toml:415, hypseus :471), it produces a redundant double-KILL backstop. Harmless today but fragile against future/custom quit_cmds, and the replace converts only the FIRST conceptual TERM family by text, not by semantics.
confirmed.impact: No data loss; potential over-broad escalation on a custom quit_cmd that contains '-TERM' in an unexpected place. Low risk given the current curated set.
confirmed.fix: Either gate the backstop on the backend declaring it needs escalation, or build the KILL command explicitly rather than by free-text replace (e.g. only escalate the pkill token).

verify.reasoning: quit-combo-watcher.py:111 `kill_cmd = quit_cmd.replace('-TERM', '-KILL')` is a whole-string substring replace, gated only on `kill_cmd != quit_cmd` (line 112). For backends whose quit_cmd ALREADY contains an explicit `pkill -KILL` escalation (controller-policy.toml:415 eden, :471 hypseus) the replace also leaves that -KILL intact, producing a redundant double-KILL backstop, and it would rewrite any incidental '-TERM' substring in a future custom quit_cmd. Genuinely harmless today (SIGKILL is idempotent; matches nothing if already dead) — this is a fragility/style note rather than a live bug. Lowering to info.

confirmed.title: load_merged parses the base controller-policy.toml with no error guard
confirmed.description: load_merged() wraps the LOCAL read in localpolicy.load (which swallows TOMLDecodeError/OSError, localpolicy.py:22) but the BASE controller-policy.toml is parsed with a bare tomllib.load (line 22), no try. A hand-edit typo in the base file therefore raises out of every policy.* / backup / splash RPC method that calls load_merged(). This is contained by rpc._run's per-method try/except (rpc.py:64) so it returns EINTERNAL rather than crashing the daemon, and the router's load_policy is likewise unguarded on the base by deliberate design (base is human-edited, hard-fail acceptable). Noted for parity awareness rather than as a fault: in the GUI the symptom is every config page failing to load with an opaque error until the base file is fixed.
confirmed.impact: A single typo in the human-edited base TOML makes the entire MAD panel's config surface return generic EINTERNAL errors (no targeted 'your policy file has a syntax error at line N' message).
confirmed.fix: Optionally catch TOMLDecodeError around the base load in load_merged() and raise an RpcError-friendly message (or surface the decode error text) so the panel can tell the user the base file is malformed instead of a blanket EINTERNAL.

verify.reasoning: lib/policy.py:22 `base = tomllib.load(POLICY.open("rb"))` is a bare load with no try, while the LOCAL read goes through localpolicy.load (localpolicy.py:19-23) which swallows TOMLDecodeError/OSError. A typo in the base controller-policy.toml therefore raises out of load_merged(). It is contained by rpc._run's try/except (rpc.py:64-67) which returns EINTERNAL rather than crashing the daemon. The finding correctly self-rates this as info/parity-awareness, not a fault. Accurate.

confirmed.title: art.resolve resolves attacker-controlled relative names with no path-traversal containment
confirmed.description: art.resolve takes names straight from the request and resolve_art joins each verbatim to the art-dir chain (base / nm, line 90) with no normalization or is-inside-base check. A name like "../../../../home/deck/.config/secret" resolves outside the art dirs and, if it is_file(), the absolute path is returned to the panel. Because the result is only handed to ImageComponent::setImage in the trusted, single-user local panel (no network input), this is a hardening note rather than an exploitable hole.

confirmed.fix: Resolve each candidate and verify the realpath stays within one of art_dirs() (Path.resolve().is_relative_to(base.resolve())) before returning it.

verify.reasoning: resolve_art (systems_cmds.py:79-93) joins each candidate verbatim `p = base / nm` (90) with no normalization or is-inside-base check, and returns str(p) if p.is_file() (91-92). art.resolve (260-270) passes request-supplied names straight in. A '../..' name would resolve outside art_dirs(). But this is a trusted, single-user local panel with no network input, and the result only feeds an image path — so info/defense-in-depth is the right rating, exactly as the finding self-rates it.

confirmed.title: Dead/duplicate JSIOCGNAME ioctl constant assignment

confirmed.title: show-launchscreen.py docstring claims a FocusOut-driven close that the code deliberately does not implement

There is no <FocusOut> binding anywhere in the file (confirmed by grep). confirmed.title: Eden/xemu blindly trust section_body parse; an existing [Controls]/[input.bindings] absence silently produces a fresh section
confirmed.description: eden_cfg.py:167 and xemu_cfg.py:100 use `inifile.section_body(text, ...) or ""`. inifile.section_body (inifile.py:14-17) returns None when the section is absent, and set_section then APPENDS a new section. This is correct behavior, but combined with the non-atomic write it means a truncated config (section header lost) would be silently rebuilt with only the router's keys, discarding the user's other [Controls] entries that lived in the now-missing section. Not a bug on its own; noted as a robustness interaction with the atomicity gap.

confirmed.fix: Primarily addressed by making writes atomic (see the atomicity finding); optionally log when an expected section is missing rather than silently creating it.

verify.reasoning: inifile.section_body (inifile.py:14-17) returns None when the section is absent; set_section (20-29) appends a new section when not found. eden_cfg.py:167 and xemu_cfg.py:100 use 'section_body(...) or ""'. The finding itself rates this info and explicitly states 'Not a bug on its own; noted as a robustness interaction with the atomicity gap' — an accurate, honest characterization. Behavior is correct on well-formed files; only matters combined with the non-atomic-write damage in 5.0. Info severity correct.

confirmed.title: wii-nav-bridge rescan re-arm cooldown only applies to slots not currently in self.readers
confirmed.description: rescan() computes `want` (discovered nodes minus the tester-claimed one) and only sets a fresh _disarmed cooldown for nodes that are NOT already in self.readers (line 295 `if node not in self.readers`). This is correct for normal acquisition, but note the comment at lines 71-76 / 232-235 frames the cooldown as protecting against the tester's '+ held' exit gesture leaking R1 into nav. When the tester releases a slot, tester_claim() returns None, last_claim changes, rescan() runs, and the previously-claimed node is genuinely re-added (it was popped from readers while claimed at line 290-291 because it was excluded from `want`), so it does get a fresh cooldown — the intended path works. The gap is only if a slot is somehow re-acquired while already present in self.readers (e.g. a transient claim flip within one tick that does not actually pop it); such a slot would skip the cooldown. This is an edge case, not a normal-use bug.
confirmed.impact: In the documented tester-exit flow the cooldown does fire, so the held-+ leak is suppressed as designed. The residual risk is only a fast claim toggle that leaves the node continuously in self.readers — then a still-held button could momentarily leak into ES-DE nav. Hard to hit in practice.
confirmed.fix: If hardening is wanted, key the cooldown on the (re)appearance of a node in `discovered` regardless of whether it was popped, or always reset _disarmed for any node that re-enters `want` after a claim change. Otherwise document that the cooldown is acquisition-time only.

The finder's own analysis is borne out by the code: in the documented tester-exit flow the claimed node was popped at lines 290-291 (excluded from `want` while claimed) so it re-enters readers and DOES get a fresh cooldown — the intended protection fires. The residual gap is only a fast claim toggle that leaves a node continuously in self.readers, which is hard to hit. This is effectively a self-refuting/info-level note: the primary documented concern works; only a hard-to-reach edge case remains. Downgrading to info given the finder concedes the normal path is correct.

confirmed.title: hypseus-pin.sh appends global-args via unquoted command substitution (intentional word-split, but unguarded against odd content)
confirmed.description: `set -- "$@" $(cat "$_gargs")` word-splits and glob-expands the contents of ~/Emulation/storage/hypseus/global-args into Hypseus argv (SC2046 is disabled deliberately because these are meant to be flags). The file is machine-written by hypinput.set_global_seek (only ever `-seek_frames_per_ms 0`), so in practice the content is safe, but there is no validation and globbing is not disabled, so a stray `*` or shell metacharacter hand-edited into the file would expand against the cwd. Low risk given the controlled writer, noted for completeness.

synthesis.risk_summary: The system is fundamentally sound, but two recurring weaknesses run through the riskiest findings. First, several batch/utility scripts that edit ES-DE gamelists or emulator config files do not protect against the things the project rules exist to prevent: they rewrite gamelist.xml with no "is ES-DE running?" check (so the user's scraping/de-dup work is silently thrown away the next time ES-DE closes), they overwrite live config files in place rather than safely (so a power-loss or kill mid-write can corrupt a controller profile or the global RetroArch settings), and a couple of them delete user media/overrides outright instead of moving them to a recoverable _TMP folder. Second, a handful of real functional bugs degrade specific features: the Model 2 lightgun crosshair toggle is completely dead (a script targets the literal text "M2CONFIGFILE" instead of the actual file), quitting the MAD panel from the camera-tuning page leaves the Sinden lightgun non-functional until a stray background process is killed by hand, and the Steam metadata fetcher crashes outright if it meets one non-standard shortcut. None of these will silently corrupt the whole system, and the long-running daemon's worst cases are hangs/leaks rather than crashes — but the data-loss and "silently did nothing" failure modes are exactly the kind a non-technical user can't diagnose, so they deserve priority. The most severe outcomes are recoverable in principle (re-scrape, restore a backup) but the user is unlikely to know how.
themes: Gamelist edits with no ES-DE-running guard: four tools (steam-fetch-metadata 10.5/9.1, dedup-disc-gamelists 11.1/9.3, skyscraper-apply 11.2/9.4, openbor-gen-gamelist 9.2) rewrite gamelist.xml while ES-DE may be open, so the work is silently discarded on ES-DE exit. Confirmed: none of the four contains any pgrep/pidof/lock check.

Non-atomic config/gamelist writes (no temp+rename): standalone emulator writers (pcsx2/cemu/xemu/eden/rpcs3 5.0/10.7), Supermodel.ini (8.1), global retroarch.cfg (8.2), per-game bezel cfgs (8.3), user calibration JSON (3.0) and the steam gamelist (10.6) all truncate-then-write the live file, so a crash/power-loss mid-write corrupts user data with no recovery copy.

Deletes that violate the never-delete rule (no recoverable _TMP move): cover/media deleted via e.unlink() (steam-fetch-metadata 9.5/11.4/12.3), the GUI-overrides file deleted via unlink() on 'reset to defaults' (mad_backup reset_local 6.1), and deck-backup.sh pruning old backups with rm (6.2).

Unguarded parse/regex .group() crashes a whole run: base policy TOML parse aborts EVERY game launch on one typo (routing 0.1/12.4), steam-fetch-metadata .group(1) crashes on one bad shortcut (12.1), openbor-fetch-media .group() crashes on one bad manifest (12.5).

Daemon resource hygiene under teardown/concurrency: ThreadPool never shut down so SIGTERM can hang and orphan a child (rpc 10.0); ffmpeg orphaned on quit-from-camera-page (4.0); unlocked shared-state read-modify-write and cache mutation (devices 0.2/10.11, systems_cmds 2.1); leaked fds/zombies (gui_sound 4.1, daphne 7.1, policy 1.3).

Restore/overwrite operations resurrect or clobber unexpected state: mad_backup do_restore merges instead of replacing and has no emulator/ES-DE-running guard (11.5/6.5), deck-restore.sh extracts over $HOME with no ES-DE check (6.6), wire-bezels writes into non-installed cores (9.0), install-bezels clobbers per-game cfgs (8.3).
top_priorities: 8.0 — model-2-emulator.sh DrawCross toggle is dead (sed targets literal "M2CONFIGFILE" not the variable). Confirmed at lines 27/30: a one-line fix that fully repairs the Model 2 lightgun crosshair behaviour. Highest value-per-effort.

10.5 / 11.1 / 11.2 / 9.2 — gamelist tools (steam-fetch-metadata, dedup-disc-gamelists, skyscraper-apply, openbor-gen-gamelist) rewrite gamelist.xml with NO ES-DE-running guard. Confirmed: no guard exists in any of the four. Add a shared 'refuse to run if ES-DE is up' check so scraping/de-dup work isn't silently lost.

4.0 — Quitting MAD from the camera-tuning page orphans ffmpeg and leaves the Sinden lightgun dead. Confirmed: quit() (line 1874) calls _cam_restore_driver() but not _cam_kill_ffmpeg(), unlike the normal exit route (1374-1376). Add the kill call (and a try/finally around mainloop).

12.1 — steam-fetch-metadata.py crashes on any non-Steam .sh (unguarded re.search(...).group(1) at line 206). One non-conforming shortcut aborts the whole pass; guard the regex like the sibling tools do.

10.0 — mad-backend never shuts down its slow-method ThreadPool (rpc.py: _POOL created line 24, stop_all_streams line 147 never calls _POOL.shutdown()), so a normal exit during a backup/probe can hang the daemon and orphan a hypseus_capture child.

9.5 / 11.4 / 12.3 — steam-fetch-metadata.py permanently deletes the existing cover (e.unlink(), lines 138/145) when no portrait is found, with no _TMP move — a network outage can wipe many covers in one pass. Move-to-_TMP instead of unlink.

5.0 / 10.7 / 8.1 / 8.2 — make the live config writers atomic (temp+rename): standalone emulator profiles, Supermodel.ini, and the global retroarch.cfg are all truncate-then-write on a game-start path, so an interrupted launch can corrupt user input config.
0.1 / 12.4 — wrap the base controller-policy.toml parse (routing.py lines 48-50) in error handling so one hand-edit typo doesn't make every game fail to launch with an opaque ES-DE error.
6.1 — mad_backup.reset_local() deletes the user's overrides file with unlink() (line 158) and no _TMP backup; 'reset to defaults' is an unrecoverable wipe with no warning.
synthesis.coverage_gaps: RUNTIME BEHAVIOUR NOT EXERCISED: this was a static read of the code. No finding was reproduced live — the concurrency/teardown items (10.0 ThreadPool hang, 0.2/10.11 unlocked cache, 2.1 lost-update, 10.1/10.8 Wii slot races, 10.9 single-flight lock) are timing-dependent and would need a running mad-backend under hotplug/pipelined load plus the actual MAD panel to confirm real-world frequency; the GUI items (4.0 ffmpeg orphan, 4.1 zombie sounds, 4.2 fd leak) need the Tk GUI run on-device with a real camera and pads — I have no display, so all on-screen/hardware behaviour is unverified by me and should be checked by the user. NOT REVIEWED IN DEPTH: the C++ side (MadBackend.cpp) that spawns and feeds the daemon was described in the architecture brief but not read, so the GUI<->backend protocol contract (e.g. whether the panel really only sends one request at a time, which several findings rely on as mitigation) is assumed, not confirmed. The ES-DE fork's own patched sources, es_systems.xml/es_systems_sorting.xml content, and the actual controller-policy.toml/.local.toml contents were not audited — several findings ('not triggered by the current base file', 1.1/1.5) depend on what's actually in those files today. The Proton wrapper scripts were sampled (supermodel, model-2) but not all emulator .sh wrappers were read line-by-line. FOLLOW-UP PASS SHOULD: (1) run mad-backend headlessly and send a SIGTERM mid-backup to confirm 10.0; (2) have the user reproduce 4.0 on-device and check for a stray ffmpeg after quitting from the camera page; (3) audit every remaining *.sh wrapper for the same non-atomic-write and unguarded-sed patterns flagged here (the ones reviewed strongly suggest the pattern repeats); (4) confirm whether the panel can ever issue concurrent config-write RPCs, which would upgrade 2.1 from low-likelihood to a real lost-update bug; (5) verify the actual contents of controller-policy.toml against findings 1.1/1.5/6.0 to see if any are already live rather than latent.

architecture:
The repo is a controller-routing + config-panel system for a Steam Deck (ES-DE/EmulationStation). Two long-lived Python entry points plus a fleet of single-purpose scripts, all rooted at /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers.

ENTRY POINTS
1. controller-router.py — the game-launch orchestrator, NOT a daemon. Invoked synchronously by controller-router-wrap.sh and by ES-DE game-start/end hooks with a mode arg (setup/cleanup/standalone/sdl-ignore/quit-cmd/collection-of/track-view/splash-collection/lightgun-rom...). For each launch it: classifies the game (lib/classify), loads policy (lib/routing.load_policy), enumerates evdev devices (lib/devices.enumerate_devices), resolves which physical pad maps to each player port (lib/routing.resolve_pins/resolve_ports), then WRITES per-game RetroArch override .cfg files (lib/retroarch_cfg.write_override) or delegates to a standalone backend writer (lib/{cemu,dolphin,pcsx2,xemu,eden,rpcs3}_cfg). cleanup mode strips its sentinel block back out. It can pop a blocking tkinter warning dialog via a subprocess (lib.warning_dialog) whose exit code (0=Proceed,1=Cancel) can abort the emulator launch.

2. mad-backend.py — a headless NDJSON-over-stdio JSON-RPC daemon spawned by ES-DE's native MAD panel (C++ MadBackend.cpp) with pipes on stdin/stdout (stderr=log). It is the backend for the GUI; it holds an exclusive flock (mad-backend.lock), sets PR_SET_PDEATHSIG, and on stdin-EOF/SIGTERM runs stop_all_streams() to ungrab every device and kill every child. lib/madsrv/rpc.py is the RPC core: @method registry, a 4-thread ThreadPoolExecutor for slow=True methods, a single _OUT_LOCK around all stdout writes, and a Stream abstraction (background daemon thread + guaranteed cleanup()) for live device/test/capture/backup feeds. Command modules under lib/madsrv/*_cmds.py register methods (devices, policy, backup, daphne, sinden, tester, systems, preview, capture, model2, backends).

router-config-gui.py (~4600 lines) is the Tk GUI that the panel historically used; it is gamepad-navigable (GamepadNav polls evdev) and now largely mirrored by the mad-backend RPC surface.

COMMUNICATION / DATA FLOW
- GUI <-> backend: newline-delimited JSON over stdio pipes (request {id,method,params} / response {id,ok,result|error} / event / stream).
- Policy: controller-policy.toml (human-edited, tomllib) deep-merged with controller-policy.local.toml (machine-written by the GUI via lib/localpolicy.dump, atomic os.replace). lib/routing.load_policy + lib/policy.load_merged are the read path; lib/madsrv/policy_cmds.py is the write path.
- Router output: per-game/per-system RetroArch .cfg under ~/.var/app/org.libretro.RetroArch/.../config/<Core>/, and standalone emulator configs (Cemu controllerN.xml, PCSX2/xemu/eden ini, rpcs3 yml, Dolphin via dolphin-wii-mode.sh, Sinden cfg).
- Env/handoff: SDL_GAMECONTROLLER_IGNORE_DEVICES_EXCEPT whitelists printed to stdout for supermodel-native.sh; XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/es-current-view file tracks the browsed collection for launch-screen resolution.
- Device identity: lib/devices walks /dev/input/event* in RA-udev order, classifies pads/mice/keyboards, and derives stable pin ids (uniq/MAC > USB-port+iface > vid:pid). SDL access (sdl_devices) is serialized process-wide by _SDL_LOCK because SDL_Init/SDL_Quit is not thread-safe.
- Many *.sh wrap emulators (often via Proton); deck-backup.sh/deck-restore.sh/deck-post-update.sh handle backups and re-applying SteamOS-update-wiped system state (udev rules, pacman pkgs, tmpfiles).
